video_title: David Dredge, Founder and CIO, Convex Strategies
gist: David Dredge (Convex Strategies CIO) explains how regulatory accounting (Basel/VaR) manufactures vast 'riskless' assets that get over-leveraged, creating a recycling chain of volatility supply (autocallables, callable/Bermudan swaption notes, covered-call ETFs) that his firm harvests as cheap convexity insurance. He argues the real systemic risk is leverage and correlation-to-one, names Japan as the most likely next trigger, and stresses that a hedge only works if it was priced attractively going in.

insights.claim: Banks fail not by taking visible risk but by applying leverage to assets that regulatory accounting (e.g. VaR) labels riskless, so the largest systemic tails sit in zero-risk-weighted assets like super-senior CDO tranches and sovereign bonds.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Watch where capital rules let institutions hold zero/low capital against levered positions; that is where the next uncapitalized blow-up is concentrated, not in nominally 'risky' books.
insights.quote: Banks go out of business leveraging that which they can account for as riskless.

insights.claim: Silicon Valley Bank was felled by zero-risk-weighted sovereign bonds held in hold-to-maturity/available-for-sale accounts whose accrual booking let it pay bonuses on positions that had already bankrupted it.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: The same hold-to-maturity unrealized-loss mechanism still sits across the banking system and is the 'clog in the arteries' that limits Fed and bank flexibility.
insights.quote: the zero risk weighted assets that put Silicon Valley Bank out of business, riskless

insights.claim: Regulators created 'rational accounting man' — a compensated agent paid to hold zero- or negative-yielding 30-year bonds that no genuine wealth-maximizer would ever buy — by letting levered bonds count as risk-reducing under Gaussian/single-period assumptions.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Price-insensitive, regulation-driven buyers dominate bond demand, so bond pricing reflects accounting incentives rather than value — a structural source of mispriced convexity.
insights.quote: rational economic man would never buy that so you need to create rational accounting man

insights.claim: Taiwanese insurers and pension funds bought duration via callable notes with embedded Bermudan swaptions and won regulatory permission to book a called 5-year structure as a 5-year bond, so when rates rose in 2022 that duration exploded toward 50 years with massive unmarked unrealized losses.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Hidden duration and hidden short-optionality in regulated insurers/pensions are a latent source of forced repricing whenever rate vol spikes.

insights.claim: UK LDI pension funds can lever gilts five times and count the position as risk-reducing, which is why such funds are willing to own negative-yielding long bonds purely to solve a duration-matching math problem irrespective of value.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Levered, value-indifferent gilt demand makes UK rates a candidate venue for cheap convexity and for forced-unwind tail events (as 2022 LDI showed).

insights.claim: Convex Strategies positions itself as a value investor in volatility that only buys convexity when dealers actively want to recycle it, rather than hunting for tail risks first and then buying options.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: The discipline of buying only what supply makes cheap, rather than chasing a thesis, is the structural edge — investors should evaluate vol strategies on whether they buy supply or chase views.
insights.quote: we buy options because the supply comes to us once we bought enough we get a pretty good idea of where the tail risks are

insights.claim: Autocallables marketed to retail as 'capital protected down to 30%' actually embed a short at-the-money put that knocks in at minus 30%, leaving the buyer exposed to the full 100% downside below that barrier.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: The misunderstood downside in retail autocallables is a persistent, mispriced source of vol supply that hedge buyers can harvest cheaply.
insights.quote: they're actually exposed to the full 100% potential downside just doesn't kick in till they get to damn 30

insights.claim: Dodd-Frank's restrictions on bank prop risk force banks to recycle structured-product volatility risk more actively than even in the pre-GFC era, enlarging the supply available to convexity buyers.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Regulation has structurally widened the flow of recyclable vol to specialist buyers — a tailwind for the convexity-harvesting business model.

insights.claim: Japanese retail depositors have for decades bought 20-year-noncall-1 callable deposits treated as one-year deposits at ~40bp versus zero, pushing Bermudan-swaption-style structures down to the retail deposit base in a way the rest of the world has not seen.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: When these deposits stop redeeming as rates rise, the system must buy vol back en masse — a structural rate-vol demand shock embedded in Japan.
insights.quote: the guy who's rolling the deposit's now 95. So 20 years is a long time to him. So he breaks the deposit and then the whole system has to go and buy the vault back

insights.claim: The conventional view that correlation goes to one when markets crash is backwards: markets crash because correlation starts going to one, since the whole levered system is built on diversification assumptions that fail together.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Monitoring correlation breakdown gives an early warning of forced de-risking before price crashes, not after.
insights.quote: When correlation starts to go to one, markets crash.

insights.claim: The real risk to hedge against is contagion — fire spreading tree-to-tree through interconnected leverage — not the idiosyncratic 'lightning strike' that diversification already handles.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Hedges should target broad correlated contagion, not single-name events; cheapest insurance lines up with where leverage and 'dry brush' are thickest.
insights.quote: if a fire spreads throughout the forest it's going to do a lot of damage right here

insights.claim: Dredge could see LTCM's P&L deteriorating from the other side of their Asian derivative trades at Bankers Trust well before their first margin call, because correlation breakdown in their book preceded the public unwind.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Counterparty P&L stress is a leading indicator of forced liquidation cascades — useful for timing convexity monetization.
insights.quote: you could see their P&L deteriorating well before the first margin call

insights.claim: The system treats the part of the forest that has gone longest without a fire as the safest, when in reality long-quiet, heavily-levered assets are the most dangerous.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Mean-reversion-of-risk logic flags the cheapest, most-crowded 'safe' assets as the best places to own convexity.
insights.quote: the system works, it says the safest part of the forest is the part that's gone the longest without a fire. We actually know that's the opposite in the real world

themes.theme: Bond bubble peak and the hunger games of issuance

insights.claim: Price-insensitive bond ownership (central-bank QE, FX reserve managers, levered pensions, risk-parity, HTM bank books) peaked at the late-2020 all-time lows in rates and vol — a bigger passive-flow problem than equity index passive investing.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Because passive bond ownership has already peaked and is unwinding, the post-2020 regime is one of structurally messier, less-supported government bond markets.
insights.quote: the passive or price insensitive market participants was a much bigger problem in bond markets and has already peaked

insights.claim: Unrealized losses at regulated institutions that hold no capital to absorb them are a 'clog in the arteries' of the banking system, because the Fed and banks will not cut hold-to-maturity positions and crystallize losses.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: This frozen overhang keeps rate volatility structurally elevated and constrains policy flexibility — a persistent backdrop for rate-vol opportunities.

insights.claim: With price stability back as an issue since 2022, government bond issuance has become a zero-sum 'hunger games' where Germany, Japan and others must fund their own bonds rather than buy each other's, escalating into trade, resource and military competition.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Bond-supply competition pressures term premia and cross-border flows globally; watch for reduced foreign sponsorship of each nation's debt.
insights.quote: literally that competition to issue bonds becomes a trade competition becomes a resource competition and built becomes build up your military competition

themes.theme: Rate-vol as the trade of the cycle (2020-2022)

insights.claim: Interest-rate volatility from mid-2020 to end-Q3 2021 was arguably the cheapest convexity in all of history — lowest term structure, lowest skew, cheapest price ever — driven by record-low yields, record passive bond ownership, and record structured-product vol supply.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Identifies the template: own convexity where supply, leverage and suppression are most extreme; rate-vol, not equity-vol, was the 2022 payoff.
insights.quote: the cheapest maybe arguably the cheapest best value in volatility most particularly in convexity vol ever in all of history

insights.claim: The 2022 drawdown was a correlation breakdown, not an equity sell-off, so the money was made in interest-rate vol (and vol-of-vol) rather than in S&P puts, which were expensive and inefficient after COVID destroyed equity-vol supply.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Hedge selection must match the nature of the shock; S&P vol failed in 2022 precisely because it was priced richly after 2020.
insights.quote: Va worked great 2022 if you owned interest rate V

insights.claim: The biggest relative change in volatility pricing in 2022 was in Japan, where the MOVE-equivalent had been crushed to roughly 10 by years of ZIRP/NIRP/QE/YCC, versus ~40 in the US — making Japanese rate-vol strips that isolated vol-of-vol near zero an exceptional insurance policy.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Suppressed-vol regimes (YCC, pegs) create the cheapest vol-of-vol; Japan rate-vol remains a prime convexity venue.
insights.quote: the equivalent in Japan because of the multiple years of zero QEQE zer nerp and YCC that equivalent in normal ratefall terms was 10

insights.claim: The 2022 rate move was triggered not by the Fed but by the RBA abandoning its 10bp three-year YCC cap at end-September 2021 with inflation already in high single digits, which then cascaded to Europe and finally blew up Japanese rate vol in March 2022.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Pegged/YCC regimes are the ignition points; watch central banks defending caps against rising inflation as the fuse for cross-market vol repricing.
insights.quote: the thing that kicked it off was when the geniuses at RBA... held on to their 10 basis point three-year bond YC cap all the way through the end of September 2021

themes.theme: Japan as the likely next trigger

insights.claim: Japan is the most likely trigger of the next systemic event because it is the world's largest foreign net creditor and the last major buyer of everyone else's bonds, so any reversal of its outbound capital flows would hit every bond market that needs to roll over debt.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Monitor Japanese outbound capital flows and yen intervention as the key tell for global bond-market stress.
insights.quote: if you push me for an opinion, I'd say it'll probably be Japan. Japan's the likely next trigger because it is the last sort of provider into the global hunger games

insights.claim: Japan runs a massive trade surplus yet must intervene against the yen, implying capital is flowing out heavily; Dredge's BOJ contact concedes Japanese inflation would be far higher absent government subsidies and repeated FX intervention.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Suppressed/managed Japanese inflation and yen mean official measures understate the pressure; a forced normalization is a fat-tailed global risk.

insights.claim: Unlike other high-debt nations, Japan's problem is purely government-sector: its corporate and household sectors are net savers who benefit from higher rates, so Japan has fiscal dominance but not the financial-system dominance (weak banks/private credit) that plagues other countries.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Japan could crowd in private-sector borrowing if government shrinks — a differentiated opportunity versus other debt-burdened economies.
insights.quote: it has the highest government debt to GDP of any modern country in history. But unlike all the other places... it does not have a private sector debt problem

themes.theme: AI buildout, single-name vol and topside structures

insights.claim: Topside (upside) equity volatility has been persistently cheap for about two years since summer 2024, driven by autocallable knockouts and covered-call/leveraged-ETF structures that suppress upside vol even as markets rally.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Cheap topside vol plus benchmark-chasing flows create melt-up risk; investors underperforming on rallies get forced to chase calls higher.
insights.quote: we've seen for really last couple years... consistent persistent cheapness of topside volatility

insights.claim: The manager hedging Korea's leveraged SK Hynix ETFs is a standing daily bid for one-week vol because the product has grown so large and volatile that it is almost impossible to hedge, and sophisticated market makers and hedge funds front-run those flows.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Korean chip leveraged-ETF hedging flows are a structural vol bid; one-week Hynix/Samsung options are an extreme, mechanically-driven market to fade or front-run carefully.
insights.quote: the guy who's managing those levered ETFs on highix... he's a good bid for one week V every day all day because it's almost impossible to manage

insights.claim: Volatility has migrated from index to single-name: Nvidia option volumes now rival or exceed S&P volumes on most days as structured products shifted toward single names over the last 2.5-3 years.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Vol opportunity and structured-supply now sit in single-name options globally, not just S&P — broadening the convexity hunting ground.
insights.quote: the volumes in Nvidia options are every bit as big as S&P volumes most days and more and more

insights.claim: Convex Strategies frames itself as the liability side of a balance sheet — paying carry to maintain sensitivity that decays daily — and treats time, not market direction, as its primary enemy.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Vol-long programs must be judged on efficiency of carry and sensitivity maintenance, not on episodic P&L; greed or selling to offset carry is what blows them up.
insights.quote: our enemy in a sense is time... if we don't do anything tomorrow, we're less sensitive than we were today

insights.claim: Monetization timing is dictated by counterparties: when dealers' risk methodology forces them to buy volatility back at extremes, that buy-back signals risk is leaving the system, so the firm sells the now-expensive insurance back to them.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: The dealer buy-back wave is both the monetization trigger and the all-clear signal — a reusable rule for when to harvest convexity gains.
insights.quote: when they're buying back, by definition, that means the risk is coming out of the system

insights.claim: The book is built in layers: lower-level, higher-probability convexity that is monetizable on small moves funds the cost of owning far-out-of-the-money wing convexity that delivers the highly asymmetric payoff in the events that truly matter.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Layered convexity is how to fund persistent deep-tail protection without bleeding to zero — a template for sustainable tail hedging.

insights.claim: Variance swaps are the ideal convex instrument but become un-sourceable precisely when vol is high, so a buyer who can cooperatively sell them back to a stuck dealer holds a structural advantage in monetization.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Relationship-driven willingness to return risk to dealers when they are short and stuck is itself an edge that improves pricing and access over cycles.
insights.quote: nobody wants to sell variant swaps because it's outside of your risk limit when you see that convexity

insights.claim: A hedge only works if it was priced attractively going in; a hedge that back-tested well in a prior cheap regime (e.g. S&P puts before 2020) fails in the next event if it has since become expensive (as in 2022).
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Evaluate any tail hedge on its entry pricing today, not on historical performance — the cheapest effective hedges typically back-test poorly.
insights.quote: if it's not priced attractively today, it won't work like it did when it was priced attractively. And more often than not, the thing that will work today doesn't back test well

insights.claim: In the August 2024 Japan-driven unwind, the best hedge was the Nikkei, not the S&P, because the Nikkei sell-off and skew change were far larger than in the S&P.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: Source-of-shock determines the best hedge venue; Japan-triggered events are best hedged in Japanese instruments, not US index vol.
insights.quote: the best hedge in that environment turned out to be Nikkeay because the Nikkay selloff was a hell of a lot bigger and the change in skew was a hell of a lot larger

insights.claim: The underlying market is a power-law distribution, but layered on top is a power-law distribution of leverage and volatility, so vol sold beyond two standard deviations is priced without capital backing the true tail while the seller still books the enhanced annual revenue.
insights.speaker: David Dredge
insights.so_what: The compounding of fat tails in both prices and leverage means far-tail options are systematically underpriced — the core rationale for owning wing convexity.
insights.quote: we all know that the underlying market is not normally distributed it's a power law distribution but then layered on top of that is a power law distribution of leverage and volatility
